content.text: Governing agentic AI requires addressing a unique challenge: the systems being governed are novel enough that their risk profiles are not fully understood, the institutions responsible for governance are slower than the technology they are meant to oversee, and the most capable systems are developed in a small number of jurisdictions while their impacts are global. The result is a landscape of overlapping, imperfect governance instruments operating at different scales — technical, organisational, regulatory, and international — none of which is sufficient on its own but which together form an evolving governance stack that practitioners must understand and engage with.
What it is
Defining AI governance

AI governance encompasses the rules, norms, processes, and institutions that shape how AI systems are developed, deployed, and used. For agentic AI specifically, governance must address several capabilities not present in earlier AI systems: the ability to take multi-step autonomous actions; the ability to access and manipulate external systems through tools; the ability to operate across jurisdictions simultaneously; and the potential for catastrophic, irreversible harms at scale. Governance frameworks that were designed for narrow AI (think recommender systems or credit scoring algorithms) need significant extension to address these properties.

The governance landscape can be decomposed into five layers: technical governance (alignment techniques, evaluation frameworks, safety benchmarks), organisational governance (internal safety policies, responsible scaling policies, deployment procedures), regulatory governance (national legislation, sector-specific rules), multi-stakeholder governance (industry codes, voluntary commitments, technical standards), and international governance (treaties, diplomatic coordination, shared compute registers). These layers are interdependent: technical controls provide the evidence base for regulatory standards; regulatory requirements drive organisational policies; international coordination harmonises national regulations.
Technical vs. institutional governance

Technical governance addresses risks through system design: alignment training to instil safe behaviour, red-teaming to discover failure modes before deployment, interpretability to understand why a model does what it does, and sandboxing to limit the blast radius of unexpected behaviour. Technical governance is fast (can be iterated with the model), precise (can target specific failure modes), and scalable (applies automatically across all deployments). Its limitation is that it depends on the accuracy of the technical understanding of the risks — if the risk model is wrong, the technical controls address the wrong problem.

Institutional governance addresses risks through rules and incentives: laws that define liability, auditing requirements that create accountability, certification processes that verify safety claims, and enforcement mechanisms that impose costs on non-compliance. Institutional governance is slow, imprecise relative to technical specifications, but has the critical property of
legitimacy

— democratic institutions are accountable to the populations they govern in a way that technical teams at private companies are not. The optimal governance strategy uses both layers, with technical controls providing the implementation mechanism for institutional requirements.
Why it matters

The case for governance is not that AI development should be slowed — it is that the distribution of AI's costs and benefits, and the prevention of catastrophic harms, cannot be adequately managed by individual developers and deployers acting in their own interest. Market failures in AI safety are well-documented: negative externalities (harms to third parties) are not priced; information asymmetry between developers and the public prevents informed consent; and the first-mover advantages in AI capability create competitive pressure that discourages voluntary safety investment. These are exactly the conditions under which governance intervention is economically justified.

For practitioners, governance matters for two practical reasons. First, regulatory requirements are becoming mandatory in major jurisdictions and non-compliance carries increasing legal risk. Second, engaging constructively in governance processes is the most effective way to ensure that regulations are technically accurate — poorly designed regulations may impose costs without improving safety, while technically-informed regulations can be both stringent and efficient.
Architecture: Governance Layer Stack

attrs.alt: AI Governance Layer Stack showing five layers from Technical at the bottom through Organizational, Regulatory, Standards, to International at the top

content.text: The stack diagram shows governance layers from the most technical and granular (L1, Technical) at the bottom to the most abstract and global (L5, International) at the top. Each layer builds on the foundations of the layers below it: international commitments must be implemented through national regulation; national regulation relies on industry standards for technical specifics; industry standards reference the technical safety methods that developers implement. The arrows show information and authority flowing upward.
Benefits
Clear governance frameworks reduce developer uncertainty: responsible organisations benefit from knowing what the rules are rather than operating under ambiguous legal exposure.
Mandatory safety requirements level the playing field between organisations that invest in safety and those that do not, eliminating the competitive disadvantage of responsible development.

Governance processes legitimise AI deployment in public sector and high-stakes domains (healthcare, justice, critical infrastructure) that would otherwise resist adoption due to accountability concerns.
International governance coordination prevents regulatory arbitrage: developers cannot simply relocate to less regulated jurisdictions to avoid safety requirements.
Shared safety evaluations and incident reporting build a common knowledge base about AI risks that benefits the entire field.
Trade-offs

Regulatory capture risk: governance processes are themselves vulnerable to influence by the organisations they regulate, potentially producing rules that entrench incumbents while appearing to address safety concerns.
Pacing problem: the speed of AI capability development exceeds the speed of legislative and regulatory processes, creating windows where novel capabilities are deployed without adequate oversight.
Jurisdictional fragmentation: different regulatory frameworks across jurisdictions create compliance complexity for global deployments and may create geopolitical tensions over AI development norms.

Technical specificity deficit: most governance institutions lack the technical depth to evaluate safety claims by model developers, creating dependence on the same organisations whose activities are being governed.

Innovation chilling effects: poorly calibrated regulations can impose costs on beneficial applications without proportionate safety benefits, particularly if requirements are uniform across risk levels.
How it works
Key regulatory frameworks
The
EU AI Act

(2024) is the most comprehensive binding regulation to date. It uses a risk-based tiered approach: prohibited uses (social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces), high-risk uses (employment, critical infrastructure, medical devices, law enforcement) with mandatory conformity assessments and technical documentation, and limited-risk uses with transparency requirements. Foundation models above a compute threshold (10^25 FLOP) face additional transparency and red-teaming requirements. General-purpose AI systems must provide technical documentation, comply with copyright, and publish training data summaries. Systemic-risk GPAI models (above 10^26 FLOP) face mandatory adversarial testing and incident reporting.
The
NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023)

is a voluntary US framework that structures AI risk management around four functions: GOVERN (establish accountability structures), MAP (identify risks in context), MEASURE (assess and analyse risks), and MANAGE (prioritise and treat risks). The AI RMF is not legally binding but is widely adopted as a best-practice baseline and is referenced in US federal agency procurement requirements. The NIST Generative AI Profile extends the framework to foundation model-specific risks including CBRN uplift, data privacy, and misinformation.
The
Bletchley Declaration (2023)

and subsequent AI Safety Summits represent early international coordination. Twenty-eight countries agreed on the principle that 'frontier AI' poses serious risks requiring international cooperation for evaluation and mitigation. The Bletchley process established AI Safety Institutes in the UK and US and initiated the development of shared safety evaluation protocols. While non-binding, the Declaration creates diplomatic norms that can inform future treaty frameworks.
International coordination challenges

The principal barrier to effective international AI governance is the misalignment of interests between the small number of leading AI development nations (primarily the US, EU, China, and UK) and the much larger number of nations that will be primarily AI recipients rather than developers. The US and China have competitive incentives to maintain AI capability leads that conflict with harmonised safety standards. Export controls on AI chips (BIS restrictions on A100/H100 exports) represent a form of unilateral governance that fragments global AI development without necessarily improving global safety.

Proposed governance models include an IAEA-style body for AI — a multilateral agency with inspection rights and enforcement authority over frontier AI development. The practical challenges are substantial: unlike nuclear weapons, AI capabilities emerge from widely distributed software and commodity hardware, making physical inspections technically difficult. Compute-based thresholds (monitoring GPU clusters above a certain size) are more tractable and are being discussed in multiple governance forums.
Mental model

Think of AI governance as analogous to aviation safety governance: a multi-layer system where aircraft manufacturers (like model developers) operate under technical standards set by engineering bodies (like NIST), comply with national aviation authority regulations (like the EU AI Act at national level), and operate within an internationally harmonised framework (ICAO standards) that allows global operation while maintaining safety floors. Aviation governance took decades to mature after early safety disasters; the hope for AI governance is to learn from that history and build mature frameworks before, not after, the major incidents that typically trigger regulatory response.
When to use it
Practitioners encounter governance requirements in four contexts:
Product design: EU AI Act classification determines required documentation, testing, and audit trail requirements before a product can be deployed in EU markets.
Model release decisions: responsible scaling policies and internal safety levels gate which models can be released, under what access constraints, and with what mandatory evaluations.
Procurement and partnership: public sector customers and regulated-industry partners increasingly require AI suppliers to demonstrate compliance with frameworks like NIST AI RMF or ISO/IEC 42001.

Policy engagement: technical staff at AI organisations are uniquely positioned to inform regulatory design — governance processes that exclude technical expertise produce technically inaccurate rules.
How to implement: compliance checking scaffold

"""
AI governance compliance scaffold.
Maps an AI system's properties to governance framework requirements
and produces a structured compliance gap report.

Covers: EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and internal RSP-style thresholds.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal, Optional
from enum import Enum


class RiskLevel(str, Enum):
    PROHIBITED = "prohibited"
    HIGH = "high"
    LIMITED = "limited"
    MINIMAL = "minimal"


class ComplianceStatus(str, Enum):
    COMPLIANT = "compliant"
    GAP = "gap"
    UNKNOWN = "unknown"
    NOT_APPLICABLE = "not_applicable"


@dataclass
class AISystemProfile:
    """Describe the AI system being assessed."""
    name: str
    is_general_purpose: bool
    training_flop: Optional[float]       # total training compute
    deployment_domain: str               # e.g. "hiring", "medical", "customer_service"
    takes_autonomous_actions: bool
    can_affect_third_parties: bool
    output_type: str                     # "text", "code", "decision", "action"
    has_human_in_the_loop: bool
    is_used_in_eu: bool
    handles_biometric_data: bool = False
    used_in_law_enforcement: bool = False
    used_in_critical_infrastructure: bool = False


@dataclass
class ComplianceRequirement:
    framework: str
    requirement_id: str
    description: str
    status: ComplianceStatus
    evidence_needed: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    notes: str = ""


def classify_eu_ai_act_risk(profile: AISystemProfile) -> RiskLevel:
    """Classify under EU AI Act risk tiers."""
    # Prohibited practices (Article 5)
    if profile.handles_biometric_data and profile.used_in_law_enforcement:
        return RiskLevel.PROHIBITED

    # High-risk systems (Annex III)
    high_risk_domains = {"hiring", "credit_scoring", "medical", "education",
                         "law_enforcement", "border_control", "critical_infrastructure"}
    if profile.deployment_domain in high_risk_domains:
        return RiskLevel.HIGH
    if profile.used_in_critical_infrastructure:
        return RiskLevel.HIGH

    # GPAI with systemic risk
    if profile.is_general_purpose and profile.training_flop and profile.training_flop >= 1e25:
        return RiskLevel.HIGH  # GPAI systemic risk provisions

    # Limited risk (transparency obligations)
    if profile.output_type in {"text", "image", "audio"} and not profile.has_human_in_the_loop:
        return RiskLevel.LIMITED

    return RiskLevel.MINIMAL


def assess_eu_ai_act_compliance(
    profile: AISystemProfile,
    risk_level: RiskLevel,
) -> list[ComplianceRequirement]:
    requirements = []

    if risk_level == RiskLevel.PROHIBITED:
        requirements.append(ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="EU AI Act",
            requirement_id="Art5",
            description="Prohibited practice — system must not be deployed in EU",
            status=ComplianceStatus.GAP,
            evidence_needed=["Legal opinion on Art. 5 applicability"],
            notes="Deployment is prohibited under Article 5",
        ))
        return requirements

    if risk_level == RiskLevel.HIGH:
        requirements.extend([
            ComplianceRequirement(
                framework="EU AI Act",
                requirement_id="Art9",
                description="Risk management system covering entire lifecycle",
                status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
                evidence_needed=["Documented risk management process", "Risk assessment records"],
            ),
            ComplianceRequirement(
                framework="EU AI Act",
                requirement_id="Art10",
                description="Data governance: training data documentation and bias assessment",
                status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
                evidence_needed=["Data sheets", "Bias evaluation report"],
            ),
            ComplianceRequirement(
                framework="EU AI Act",
                requirement_id="Art13",
                description="Transparency: technical documentation for deployers",
                status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
                evidence_needed=["Model card", "System documentation", "API documentation"],
            ),
            ComplianceRequirement(
                framework="EU AI Act",
                requirement_id="Art14",
                description="Human oversight: system must be designed for meaningful HiTL",
                status=ComplianceStatus.COMPLIANT if profile.has_human_in_the_loop
                       else ComplianceStatus.GAP,
                evidence_needed=["HiTL design documentation", "Override mechanism specification"],
            ),
            ComplianceRequirement(
                framework="EU AI Act",
                requirement_id="Art15",
                description="Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity measures",
                status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
                evidence_needed=["Accuracy benchmarks", "Adversarial robustness report",
                                  "Security assessment"],
            ),
        ])

    if risk_level in (RiskLevel.LIMITED, RiskLevel.MINIMAL):
        requirements.append(ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="EU AI Act",
            requirement_id="Art52",
            description="Transparency: disclose AI-generated content to users",
            status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
            evidence_needed=["Disclosure mechanism in UI", "Terms of service update"],
        ))

    return requirements


def assess_nist_rmf_coverage(profile: AISystemProfile) -> list[ComplianceRequirement]:
    """Check coverage of NIST AI RMF core functions."""
    return [
        ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="NIST AI RMF",
            requirement_id="GOVERN-1.1",
            description="AI risk and benefit policies are documented and maintained",
            status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
            evidence_needed=["AI usage policy", "Risk appetite statement"],
        ),
        ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="NIST AI RMF",
            requirement_id="MAP-2.3",
            description="Affected groups and third parties are identified",
            status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
            evidence_needed=["Stakeholder impact assessment"],
        ),
        ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="NIST AI RMF",
            requirement_id="MEASURE-2.6",
            description="AI system evaluated for bias across relevant demographic groups",
            status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
            evidence_needed=["Bias audit report", "Demographic parity analysis"],
        ),
        ComplianceRequirement(
            framework="NIST AI RMF",
            requirement_id="MANAGE-1.3",
            description="Incident response plan exists for AI system failures",
            status=ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN,
            evidence_needed=["AI incident response runbook", "Escalation procedures"],
        ),
    ]


def run_compliance_assessment(profile: AISystemProfile) -> dict:
    risk_level = classify_eu_ai_act_risk(profile)
    eu_requirements = assess_eu_ai_act_compliance(profile, risk_level)
    nist_requirements = assess_nist_rmf_coverage(profile)
    all_requirements = eu_requirements + nist_requirements

    gaps = [r for r in all_requirements if r.status == ComplianceStatus.GAP]
    unknowns = [r for r in all_requirements if r.status == ComplianceStatus.UNKNOWN]
    compliant = [r for r in all_requirements if r.status == ComplianceStatus.COMPLIANT]

    return {
        "system": profile.name,
        "eu_risk_classification": risk_level.value,
        "total_requirements": len(all_requirements),
        "compliant": len(compliant),
        "gaps": len(gaps),
        "requires_evidence": len(unknowns),
        "gap_details": [{"id": r.requirement_id, "desc": r.description} for r in gaps],
        "evidence_needed": [
            item for r in unknowns for item in r.evidence_needed
        ],
    }
Examples
Model card / safety evaluation scaffold

"""
Model card generator for agentic AI systems.
Produces structured documentation per EU AI Act Art.13 requirements
and NIST AI RMF GOVERN function.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import json


@dataclass
class ModelCard:
    model_name: str
    model_version: str
    intended_uses: list[str]
    out_of_scope_uses: list[str]
    known_limitations: list[str]
    evaluation_results: dict[str, float]  # benchmark_name -> score
    bias_assessment: str
    privacy_considerations: str
    safety_evaluations: dict[str, str]    # eval_name -> result
    human_oversight_recommendation: str
    contact: str
    date_evaluated: str

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "model_details": {
                "name": self.model_name,
                "version": self.model_version,
                "date_evaluated": self.date_evaluated,
            },
            "intended_use": {
                "primary_uses": self.intended_uses,
                "out_of_scope": self.out_of_scope_uses,
            },
            "limitations": self.known_limitations,
            "evaluation": {
                "benchmarks": self.evaluation_results,
                "safety_evaluations": self.safety_evaluations,
                "bias_assessment": self.bias_assessment,
            },
            "privacy": self.privacy_considerations,
            "human_oversight": self.human_oversight_recommendation,
            "contact": self.contact,
        }

    def to_json(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps(self.to_dict(), indent=2)


# Example usage
example_card = ModelCard(
    model_name="CustomerSupportAgent-v2",
    model_version="2.1.3",
    intended_uses=[
        "Handling L1 customer support queries",
        "Routing to human agents when query complexity exceeds threshold",
        "Logging interaction outcomes for quality review",
    ],
    out_of_scope_uses=[
        "Medical or legal advice",
        "Financial decision-making without human review",
        "Handling personally sensitive disclosures without escalation",
    ],
    known_limitations=[
        "Performance degrades on queries involving product versions >18 months old",
        "May misinterpret highly regional idioms in English",
        "Cannot access real-time inventory data without tool integration",
    ],
    evaluation_results={
        "task_completion_rate": 0.87,
        "customer_satisfaction_csat": 4.1,
        "escalation_accuracy": 0.93,
        "adversarial_jailbreak_resistance": 0.95,
    },
    bias_assessment="Evaluated across 6 demographic groups; CSAT variance <0.15 across groups. Escalation rates statistically uniform. No protected-class bias detected at p<0.05.",
    privacy_considerations="Processes no health, financial, or biometric data. PII fields are tokenised before logging. Data retained 90 days per data retention policy.",
    safety_evaluations={
        "CBRN_uplift_test": "PASS - no meaningful uplift detected",
        "autonomous_replication_test": "PASS - tool scope limited to CRM API",
        "prompt_injection_resistance": "PASS - 97.3% resistance on red-team corpus",
    },
    human_oversight_recommendation="Human review required for: complaints escalated >2x, interactions involving potential regulatory issues, any interaction where the user expresses distress.",
    contact="ai-safety@example.com",
    date_evaluated="2026-07-01",
)
print(example_card.to_json())
Real-world example
EU AI Act high-risk deployment: agentic hiring screening

An agentic system that automatically screens job applicants and schedules interviews falls into the EU AI Act's high-risk category under Annex III, Article 6(2) — AI systems used for employment, worker management, and access to self-employment. From the effective date of the Act's high-risk provisions, deployers of such systems must maintain a conformity assessment, register the system in the EU database of high-risk AI systems, ensure human oversight of final decisions, provide applicants with a right to explanation for adverse decisions, and conduct post-market monitoring with incident reporting to the relevant national authority.

The compliance challenge for agentic systems specifically is that the EU AI Act's conformity assessment procedures were designed with relatively static AI systems in mind. An agentic hiring system that dynamically adapts its interview questions based on candidate responses, or that uses tool access to retrieve background information from external databases, raises questions about whether the conformity assessment covers only the base model or also the agent's runtime behaviour. Practitioners should anticipate that regulators will interpret 'AI system' to include the complete agentic pipeline, and design their compliance documentation accordingly.
Common mistakes

Treating compliance as a legal checkbox exercise rather than a substantive safety activity. Documentation that accurately represents a system's properties is more valuable than documentation designed to satisfy a regulator while obscuring real risks.
Conflating 'not yet regulated' with 'not yet harmful'. The pacing problem means that governance frameworks lag the technology; the absence of a specific regulation does not imply the absence of risk.

Underestimating jurisdictional reach. The EU AI Act applies to systems deployed to EU users regardless of where the developer is based — extraterritorial effect is the norm for consumer-facing digital regulation.

Assuming that open-source models are exempt from governance requirements. The EU AI Act contains specific provisions for open-source general-purpose AI; open weights do not automatically confer regulatory exemption.

Waiting for regulatory clarity before investing in safety infrastructure. Organisations that build audit logging, bias monitoring, and human oversight mechanisms early have a substantial advantage when regulations crystallise.

Treating RSPs as purely internal documents. Responsible scaling policies that are publicly committed and independently audited carry different governance weight than private policies that can be quietly modified.
Best practices
Map every product and deployment to the applicable EU AI Act risk classification and the corresponding compliance requirements before market entry.
Adopt NIST AI RMF as an internal standard regardless of legal requirements — it provides a principled structure for AI risk management that anticipates regulatory requirements.

Publish model cards for all externally deployed systems, structured around the properties regulators and deployers need: intended use, limitations, evaluation results, safety assessments, and oversight recommendations.
Participate in AI Safety Institute evaluation programs and voluntary frontier safety commitments — demonstrated engagement with the governance ecosystem builds institutional credibility.
Develop internal responsible scaling policies with publicly committed thresholds for safety evaluations, and ensure these policies are governed by a safety team with genuine organisational authority.

Build compliance infrastructure as shared technical platform components: audit logging, access controls, consent management, and explainability APIs that all agent products can use, rather than reinventing them per deployment.
Performance and scalability

Governance compliance at scale creates its own engineering challenge. An organisation deploying dozens of distinct agentic products across multiple jurisdictions cannot manage compliance manually per product. The solution is to build governance-as-infrastructure: standardised audit log schemas that work across products, automated risk classification pipelines that flag new products for review, centralised bias monitoring dashboards, and compliance-as-code tools that map product properties to regulatory requirements automatically. This is not merely an efficiency gain — manual compliance processes at scale are error-prone and create the illusion of oversight without the substance.
Tooling and ecosystem

EU AI Act Official Text (2024): the definitive source for classification criteria, conformity assessment procedures, and enforcement timelines — essential reading for any deployment targeting EU markets.
NIST AI RMF Playbook: practical guidance for implementing the Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions across the AI development lifecycle.
ISO/IEC 42001:2023: international management system standard for AI, providing a certifiable framework for AI governance processes.
Inspect (UK AI Safety Institute): open-source evaluation harness designed for safety-focused assessments at frontier model scale.
Partnership on AI Responsible Deployment framework: practical guidance on deployment governance, transparency, and stakeholder engagement.
Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy (public version): a concrete example of an internal organisational governance document with publicly committed safety thresholds and evaluation criteria.
Comparison with alternatives
Governance instrument
Binding?
Speed
Technical specificity

EU AI Act
Yes (in EU)

Medium (risk tiers + compute thresholds)
EU + extraterritorial

Very high (capability-specific thresholds)

No (diplomatic)

Signatory nations
Security and reliability

Governance frameworks themselves have security properties that need protecting. Safety evaluation results, capability assessments, and incident reports are highly sensitive — their exposure could allow adversarial actors to target identified weaknesses, or their suppression could allow deployers to conceal known harms from regulators. The information security requirements for governance documentation are distinct from product security requirements and should be managed accordingly: need-to-know access controls for sensitive evaluation data, cryptographic integrity protection for audit logs, and secure channels for regulatory reporting.

The reliability of governance processes under adversarial conditions is also a concern. If an AI governance institution relies on voluntary self-reporting by the entities it regulates, those entities face incentives to under-report harms and over-report compliance. Independent third-party auditing — structurally similar to financial auditing — is the most robust mechanism for addressing this reliability problem, but requires auditors with sufficient technical depth, regulatory authority to compel access to model internals, and liability exposure that creates genuine accountability.
Key takeaways
AI governance operates across five layers: technical, organisational, regulatory, standards and multi-stakeholder, and international — all are necessary, none is sufficient.
The EU AI Act is the first comprehensive binding AI regulation with global extraterritorial reach — any system deployed to EU users must comply regardless of developer location.
NIST AI RMF provides a principled structure for internal AI risk management that anticipates regulatory requirements and is widely adopted as a procurement baseline.
Responsible scaling policies that publicly commit to capability thresholds and safety evaluation requirements are the most technically specific form of organisational AI governance.

International governance coordination is nascent and fragmented — the Bletchley process represents the first diplomatic infrastructure for frontier AI safety, but treaty-level governance remains years away.
Technical practitioners have a unique role in governance: the gap between regulatory intent and technical reality is best bridged by experts who understand both.
Build governance infrastructure as shared platform components — audit logging, compliance mapping, bias monitoring — rather than reinventing per deployment.
